items.rationale: He views sum-check as THE technique to unlock the prover-time bottleneck because it minimizes the prover's cryptographic commitment costs; he expects good hardware implementations to follow.
items.age_note: Consistent thesis from 2022-12-28 through 2023-08-14; most recent statements reinforce, not reverse it.

citations.quote: the scalability bottleneck for awhile now has been prover time. And the right technique to unlock this is sum-check
sum-check-based SNARKs remain the most promising for minimizing total prover work, and that good hardware implementations will follow

items.rationale: His own new works; he believes they offer a new zkVM paradigm, are an order of magnitude faster than existing lookups, and make SNARKs simpler and more auditable.

citations.quote: Introducing Lasso and Jolt, two new works that I hope will change how SNARKs are designed and built
benchmarks indicate the initial Lasso implementation is about 10x faster than Halo2's lookup argument, and we expect 30x-40x after optimization

items.rationale: Most recent enthusiasm: he thinks Diamond & Posen's fine-tuning reshapes hashing-based SNARKs, amplifies recursion for Brakedown/Ligero, and pairs well with Lasso/Jolt over small/binary fields.
items.age_note: Newest excitement (2023-11-20), building on 2023-08-31 optimism about Brakedown improvements.

citations.quote: work just unveiled by Ben Diamond and Jim Posen (D&P) that, in my view, reshapes the landscape for hashing-based SNARKs
I'm excited about this approach to polynomial commitments and hopeful we'll see further improvements soon

items.rationale: Combining D&P's small-value commitments with sum-check SNARKs like Lasso/Jolt opens a path to much faster proving of widely-used hashes, with a downstream domino effect.

citations.quote: this paves the way for much faster SNARKs for standard hash functions like Keccak. The domino effect?

items.rationale: He sees ML inference (repeated small-integer matrix multiplication) as a near-term application amenable to super-efficient circuit-free protocols with night-and-day prover gains.
items.age_note: Stated once on 2022-12-31 and never restated in the corpus; ~12 months old, treat as persisted not current emphasis.

citations.quote: One that I think we'll see soon is neural network training/inference, the bottleneck in which is often repeated matrix multiplication of small integers

items.rationale: Foundational long-term thesis that SNARK-powered rollups are transformative for trust and scalability; the entire body of his work is aimed at making this practical.
items.age_note: Framing thesis stated 2022-08 to 2022-11; continually operationalized through 2023 technical work.

citations.quote: I believe blockchains will transform society's mechanisms for establishing trust & privacy, and that they raise deep technical and intellectual questions
I think this technology can be transformative

items.rationale: He argues known attacks on FRI at 80 bits run non-interactively are feasible today, securing hundreds of millions in assets — a systemic risk; he singles out dYdX's StarkEx as still at 80 bits.
items.age_note: Reaffirmed 2023-04-18 that dYdX StarkEx 'remains at 80 bits of security today'; most recent stance, not reversed.

citations.quote: Known attacks on FRI when run non-interactively at 80 bits of security are feasible. This is not an academic question.
The dYdX StarkEx verifier remains at 80 bits of security today.

items.rationale: He worries the FRI verifier-cost-vs-security tension gets resolved toward performance, eroding trust and risking misappropriated funds if the industry under-prices security.

citations.quote: if we prioritize performance over security, we may cause people to lose trust in the technology and potentially see misappropriated funds
With FRI, there is a tension between verifier costs and security... this tension may get resolved in favor of performance over security

items.rationale: Composed SNARKs have heuristic or imperfectly-understood security, with concrete loss possible at depth; deploying Poseidon at ~95 bits is premature relaxation in his view.
items.age_note: Stated 2022-12 to 2023; not contradicted later, though he hopes the hash-function situation improves.

citations.quote: while projects are deploying Poseidon with at most 95 bits of security, it's premature to relax on this
I remain cautious about security especially at large depth

items.rationale: He argues FRI provers are bottlenecked by Merkle-hashing and FFTs, and large 251/252-bit fields impose large slowdowns; he sees current FRI parameter/field choices as costly and often sub-optimal.
items.age_note: Most developed in the 2023-08/09 threads; consistent with earlier 2022 critiques.

citations.quote: current experiments with FRI-based SNARKs already show that Merkle-hashing is by far the bottleneck on the small circuits people are using, not FFTs
using the 252-bit field is resulting in a 33x slowdown for the prover

items.rationale: He warns that breaking computations into the tiniest pieces maximizes recursion depth, which is inherently sequential and un-parallelizable, becoming the real latency bottleneck for rollup scaling.

citations.quote: The real sequential bottleneck to be wary of is deep recursion. You can't parallelize that

risk_regime.rationale: Thaler is a research partner who comments exclusively on SNARK cryptography, performance, and security. The corpus contains no asset-price, market-cycle, or capital-allocation positioning, and every substantive thread carries an explicit 'not investment advice' disclaimer. His posture is technological (long-term optimist on SNARK/blockchain tech, near-term security hawk), not a risk-on/risk-off market stance.

notes: This corpus is ~95% deep technical content (sum-check, Lasso/Jolt, FRI, polynomial commitments, Brakedown/Binius, MSM cost accounting) rather than market commentary, so 'sectors' are rendered as research/technology areas. No tokens are discussed in any investment sense — projects named (StarkWare/StarkNet, Polygon zkEVM, RISC Zero, Matter Labs, dYdX) appear only as technical case studies, mostly as security/performance concerns (notably dYdX at 80-bit security), not as token bets, so both token arrays are empty. The dominant, most-recent (late-2023) thrust is a coherent two-sided thesis: strong excitement that sum-check-based proving (Lasso/Jolt) plus hashing-based commitments over small fields (Diamond-Posen/Binius) will reconfigure SNARK design for prover speed, paired with persistent concern that deployed systems under-price security (80-bit FRI, ~95-bit Poseidon, deep recursion). No major stance reversals were found; his small-fields-vs-elliptic-curves view is nuanced ('it's a mix') rather than contradictory across dates.
